prompt: What is the core governance purpose of ServiceNow Store and app management for platform owners?
choices: Control app intake, risk review, and lifecycle ownership before installation and updates
Install every available app to accelerate feature adoption
Allow business teams to deploy Store apps directly to production
Disable version tracking for simpler administration
explanation: Store governance ensures only approved, supportable apps enter the platform lifecycle.

prompt: How should a platform owner evaluate third-party Store apps before approval?
choices: Assess security posture, dependency impact, upgrade compatibility, and support model fit
Approve based only on popularity ratings in the Store
Skip compatibility checks if installation succeeds in development
Delegate all evaluation to requester teams without governance
explanation: App approval requires technical, operational, and security due diligence beyond basic functionality.

prompt: Which practice best controls Store app upgrade risk across environments?
choices: Use staged rollout with documented test evidence and rollback plans before production
Upgrade directly in production to minimize cycle time
Rely on vendor release notes without internal validation
Disable application version governance during fiscal close
explanation: Staged rollout validates compatibility and prevents broad impact from unexpected regressions.

prompt: What inventory artifact most improves governance for installed apps and plugins?
choices: A maintained catalog of app versions, owners, dependencies, and approval status
A list of app names without version details
A one-time screenshot of installed plugins
An informal channel thread with installation notes
explanation: Structured inventory enables impact analysis, upgrade planning, and ownership accountability.

prompt: Which KPI best reflects effective Store and app governance outcomes?
choices: High percentage of approved apps with successful upgrades and low post-release incidents
Maximum number of apps installed per quarter
Longest average approval turnaround regardless risk
Most customizations applied to vendor apps
explanation: Governance success combines controlled adoption with stable operations after deployment.

prompt: What is the first principle of capacity planning for ServiceNow platform governance?
choices: Forecast demand using usage trends, business growth, and workload criticality
Scale only after sustained production incidents occur
Assume historical peak loads will never repeat
Plan capacity by counting active update sets only
explanation: Capacity planning starts with evidence-based forecasting tied to business demand and service criticality.

prompt: Which signal most clearly indicates emerging capacity risk in enterprise instances?
choices: Increasing transaction latency and queue backlog during expected demand windows
Higher number of catalog items published
More dashboard widgets created monthly
Growth in knowledge article attachments
explanation: Latency and backlog under normal peaks are direct early warnings of constrained capacity.

prompt: How should professional governance address competing capacity demands from multiple product teams?
choices: Use a prioritization framework based on business criticality, SLA impact, and risk
Allocate resources equally regardless service importance
Reserve all capacity for emergency change work
Allow teams to self-assign infrastructure allocations
explanation: Risk-based prioritization aligns finite capacity with enterprise outcomes and operational commitments.

prompt: What governance artifact best supports repeatable capacity planning reviews?
choices: A capacity model with baseline metrics, demand forecasts, and trigger thresholds
A one-time architecture presentation deck
An ad hoc list of performance incidents only
Team-specific planning notes without shared definitions
explanation: A formal capacity model enables consistent review, escalation, and decision-making across teams.

prompt: Which outcome demonstrates mature capacity planning governance?
choices: Predictable peak performance with fewer emergency scaling actions and sustained SLA attainment
Higher volume of manual ticket rerouting during surges
Frequent unplanned release delays due to performance uncertainty
Increasing backlog of unresolved platform bottlenecks
explanation: Mature governance reduces reactive firefighting by making capacity decisions proactive and measurable.

prompt: What is the most important principle for governing integration throughput at enterprise scale?
choices: Set throughput budgets and backpressure controls per integration domain
Allow unlimited throughput from all sources at all times
Treat retry storms as acceptable during peak windows
Use synchronous calls for every high-volume transaction
explanation: Throughput governance requires explicit limits and controls to protect platform stability under load.

prompt: Which pattern best preserves reliability when inbound integration volume spikes unexpectedly?
choices: Queue ingestion with throttling, retry policies, and dead-letter routing for failed messages
Bypass all validation to maximize immediate ingestion
Disable monitoring until volume normalizes
Drop duplicate messages without idempotency checks
explanation: Queue-based flow control and fault handling maintain consistent throughput without sacrificing integrity.

prompt: How should architects evaluate throughput risks during new integration onboarding?
choices: Profile expected payload rates, concurrency behavior, and downstream dependency limits before go-live
Assume partner systems match internal processing capacity
Rely only on functional testing with low sample data
Enable all connectors at maximum polling frequency by default
explanation: Throughput-safe onboarding requires capacity-aware validation of real traffic and downstream constraints.

prompt: Which KPI combination best reflects integration throughput governance quality?
choices: Stable processing latency, low throttling exceptions, and sustained success rate at target volume
Maximum number of integrations regardless error trends
Highest single-batch payload size processed
Fewest documented runbooks for on-call teams
explanation: Throughput governance quality is measured by controlled performance and reliability under expected demand.

prompt: What governance action best prevents recurring throughput incidents after remediation?
choices: Codify throughput SLOs and automated alerts into release and integration acceptance criteria
Close incidents without updating operational standards
Turn off alerts once immediate issue is resolved
Move all integrations to manual batch upload
explanation: Institutionalizing SLO-driven controls prevents regressions and keeps reliability requirements enforceable.
